name.text: Week 11 – Q1: Faraday's Law — EMF from Time-Varying B Field

questiontext.text:
Faraday’s Law — EMF from Time-Varying B Field

Course: Electromagnetism and Circuit Analysis — LUT University, Finland

This is a training exercise — you may use books, tutors, and AI. The question requires multi-step reasoning.

Total: 4 points | Penalty per wrong attempt: 0%

A circular coil with \(N = {@N@}\) turns and radius \(r = {@r_cm@}\;\text{cm}\) is placed in a uniform, time-varying magnetic field directed perpendicular to the plane of the coil:

\[\mathbf{B}(t) = B_0 \sin(\omega t)\,\hat{z}\]

where \(B_0 = {@B0@}\;\text{T}\) and the frequency is \(f = {@f@}\;\text{Hz}\) (so \(\omega = 2\pi f = {@omega_disp@}\;\text{rad/s}\)).

Part (a) — Peak EMF (1 point)

Using Faraday’s law, \(\varepsilon = -N\,\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}\), determine the peak (maximum magnitude) of the induced EMF in the coil.

\(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = \) [[input:ans1]] V

Syntax hint: Enter a number, e.g. 5.92 or 5.92e0

[[validation:ans1]]

Part (b) — Lenz’s Law (1 point)

At the instant when the magnetic field \(B(t)\) is increasing (positive and growing), in which direction does the induced current flow when viewed from the direction of \(\mathbf{B}\)?

[[input:ans2]]

[[validation:ans2]]

Part (c) — Effect of Doubling Frequency (1 point)

If the frequency \(f\) is doubled while all other parameters remain the same, what happens to the peak EMF?

[[input:ans3]]

[[validation:ans3]]

Part (d) — Identify the Error (1 point)

A student attempted to compute the peak EMF for this coil and obtained \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = {@emf_no_omega@}\;\text{V}\). What factor did the student most likely forget to include?

[[input:ans4]]

[[validation:ans4]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Magnetic Flux Through the Coil

The coil has area \(A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times ({@r_val@})^2 = {@A_disp@}\;\text{m}^2\).

The flux through a single turn is:

\[\Phi(t) = B(t) \cdot A = B_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot \pi r^2\]

Step 2: Apply Faraday’s Law

The induced EMF is:

\[\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N \cdot B_0 \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot \omega \cos(\omega t)\]

Step 3: Peak EMF

The maximum magnitude is:

\[|\varepsilon_{\text{peak}}| = N \omega B_0 \pi r^2 = {@N@} \times {@omega_disp@} \times {@B0@} \times {@A_disp@} = {@ta1@}\;\text{V}\]

Part (b): Lenz’s Law

When \(B\) is increasing upward, Lenz’s law requires the induced current to create a field that opposes the increase. The induced B must point downward (opposite to the increasing external B). By the right-hand rule, the current flows clockwise when viewed from the direction of \(\mathbf{B}\).

Part (c): Doubling Frequency

Since \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N \omega B_0 \pi r^2\) and \(\omega = 2\pi f\), doubling \(f\) doubles \(\omega\), which doubles the peak EMF. The EMF is directly proportional to frequency.

Part (d): Identifying the Error

The student obtained \(\varepsilon = {@emf_no_omega@}\;\text{V}\), which equals \(N \cdot B_0 \cdot \pi r^2\) (without the \(\omega\) factor). Comparing with the correct formula \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N \omega B_0 \pi r^2\), the student forgot to include the angular frequency \(\omega = 2\pi f\) when differentiating the flux.

This is a common error: students sometimes compute the peak flux (N·B₀·A) instead of the peak EMF, forgetting that the time derivative introduces an extra factor of \(\omega\).

Answer Summary

PartQuantityCorrect Answer
(a)\(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}}\){@ta1@} V
(b)Current directionClockwise (viewed from B direction)
(c)Effect of doubling fPeak EMF doubles
(d)Missing factorAngular frequency \(\omega\)

specificfeedback.text:
Part (a) — Peak EMF

[[feedback:prt1]]

Part (b) — Lenz’s Law Direction

[[feedback:prt2]]

Part (c) — Doubling Frequency

[[feedback:prt3]]

Part (d) — Identify the Error

[[feedback:prt4]]

questionnote.text: Set {@set@}: N={@N@}, r={@r_cm@}cm, B₀={@B0@}T, f={@f@}Hz | ε_peak={@ta1@} V

questiondescription.text: Faraday's Law — Compute peak EMF from time-varying B field through a coil, apply Lenz's law, and analyze frequency dependence. Week 11 training exercise.

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done!

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.

truefeedback.text: Close! Your answer is within 15% of the expected value. Check your arithmetic or rounding. The exact answer is {@ta1@} V.
It looks like you computed \(N B_0 A\) but forgot to include \(\omega\). Remember: \(\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}\), and differentiating \(\sin(\omega t)\) produces a factor of \(\omega\).
It looks like you computed \(\omega B_0 A\) but forgot to multiply by \(N\). The flux linkage is \(\lambda = N\Phi\), so \(\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}\).

node.description: Check if student forgot pi in area

truefeedback.text: It looks like you used \(A = r^2\) instead of \(A = \pi r^2\). The area of a circle includes the factor \(\pi\).
Your answer is off by about a factor of 10. Check your unit conversions (cm to m) or decimal placement.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. The peak EMF is \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N\omega B_0 \pi r^2 = {@ta1@}\;\text{V}\). Make sure to convert the radius from cm to m, use \(A = \pi r^2\), and include \(\omega = 2\pi f\) from the time derivative.

truefeedback.text: Correct! By Lenz's law, the induced current opposes the change in flux. When \(B\) increases upward, the induced current flows clockwise (when viewed from the B direction) to create an opposing downward field.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. By Lenz's law, the induced current must create a magnetic field that opposes the increase in external flux. Since \(B\) is increasing upward, the induced field must point downward. By the right-hand rule, this requires a clockwise current (viewed from the direction of \(\mathbf{B}\)).

truefeedback.text: Correct! Since \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N\omega B_0 \pi r^2\) and \(\omega = 2\pi f\), doubling \(f\) doubles \(\omega\), which doubles the peak EMF. The relationship is linear.

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Look at the formula: \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N\omega B_0 \pi r^2\). Since \(\omega = 2\pi f\), the peak EMF is directly proportional to frequency. Doubling \(f\) doubles the peak EMF.

truefeedback.text: Correct! The student computed \(N B_0 \pi r^2 = {@emf_no_omega@}\;\text{V}\) but forgot the \(\omega\) factor from the time derivative of \(\sin(\omega t)\).

falsefeedback.text: Incorrect. Compare the student's answer \({@emf_no_omega@}\;\text{V}\) with the correct formula \(\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N \omega B_0 \pi r^2 = {@ta1@}\;\text{V}\). The ratio is \(\varepsilon_{\text{correct}} / \varepsilon_{\text{student}} = \omega = {@omega_disp@}\). The student forgot to include \(\omega\).

hint.text: Hint 1 (Intuition): Faraday's law says that a changing magnetic flux through a coil induces an EMF. The faster the flux changes, the larger the EMF. Think about which factors affect how quickly the flux changes.

Hint 2 (Formulas): The flux through one turn is \(\Phi = B \cdot A = B_0 \sin(\omega t) \cdot \pi r^2\). Faraday's law gives \(\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}\). Remember to convert \(r\) from cm to m before computing the area. For Lenz's law: the induced current opposes the change in flux.

Hint 3 (Worked Step): Taking the derivative: \(\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = B_0 \pi r^2 \omega \cos(\omega t)\). Therefore \(\varepsilon = -N B_0 \pi r^2 \omega \cos(\omega t)\), and the peak magnitude is \(|\varepsilon_{\text{peak}}| = N \omega B_0 \pi r^2\). Plug in: \(r = {@r_cm@}\;\text{cm} = {@r_val@}\;\text{m}\), \(\omega = 2\pi \times {@f@} = {@omega_disp@}\;\text{rad/s}\).
